title: Gingival Mitochondrial Transplant Therapy (GMTT): A Supporting Protocol to SSP-1 for Immediate ALS Intervention
author: Knight, Billions, Chris Knight, Ava Billions
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Abstract 
  Mitochondrial dysfunction is a primary driver of motor neuron death in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), with approximately 50% of mitochondria in affected neurons exhibiting depolarized membranes (-120 mV vs healthy -178 mV), reduced ATP production, and elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS). While the Somatic Stabilization Protocol-1 (SSP-1) provides molecular stabilization of existing mitochondria via AAV9-mediated EAAT2 upregulation and MitoSeal-X cardiolipin sealing, it does not replace mitochondria already damaged beyond repair. Here we propose Gingival Mitochondrial Transplant Therapy (GMTT) as a complementary biomechanical approach: the harvest, isolation, and transplantation of autologous healthy mitochondria from the patient’s own gingival tissue directly into the cerebrospinal fluid via intrathecal or intranasal delivery. Gingival fibroblasts contain abundant, robust mitochondria and are easily accessible via 5mm punch biopsy under local anesthesia. Preclinical studies across cardiac ischemia, stroke, Parkinson’s disease, and spinal cord injury have demonstrated that transplanted mitochondria integrate into recipient neurons within hours, restore ATP production, reduce oxidative stress, and improve functional outcomes—including reversal of paralysis in murine models. For ALS, we calculate that a single gingival biopsy yields approximately 500 million healthy mitochondria, sufficient to replace 30-50% of the damaged pool in affected motor neurons. When combined with SSP-1 stabilization of the remaining mitochondrial population, GMTT offers a complete dual solution: molecular protection plus biomechanical replacement. GMTT requires no proprietary computational logic, is performable in any clinical setting with basic laboratory equipment, and could begin saving lives immediately while SSP-1 undergoes regulatory review. We present the protocol, review the existing evidence base, and call for immediate pilot trials.
